name: architecture
context: MCP protocol architecture — hosts, clients, servers, JSON-RPC layers, lifecycle, and capability negotiation
info: This context node explains the full Model Context Protocol architecture. MCP is a stateful JSON-RPC 2.0 protocol with two layers: the data layer (lifecycle management, primitives, notifications) and the transport layer (stdio, Streamable HTTP). The architecture follows a host/client/server model where AI applications act as hosts, creating MCP client instances that maintain dedicated connections to MCP servers. Each server declares capabilities during initialization. The protocol supports real-time notifications for dynamic tool/resource/prompt updates. Understanding MCP architecture is essential before building servers or clients.
triggers: what are mcp hosts clients and servers
body:
# MCP Architecture

The Model Context Protocol is a stateful JSON-RPC 2.0 protocol with a layered architecture. Understanding these layers is critical for building servers and clients that interoperate correctly.

## Participants

MCP defines three roles:

### Host
The AI application that orchestrates everything. Examples: Claude Desktop, VS Code with Copilot, Cursor, ChatGPT. The host creates and manages one or more MCP clients.

### Client
A protocol-level component inside the host. Each client maintains a dedicated 1:1 connection with a single MCP server. The client handles JSON-RPC framing, capability tracking, and primitive discovery.

### Server
A program that exposes [[mcp/server/tools]], [[mcp/server/resources]], and [[mcp/server/prompts]] to connected clients. Servers can be local processes (stdio) or remote services (Streamable HTTP).

```
Host (Claude Desktop)
  ├── Client 1 ──stdio───── Server A (filesystem)
  ├── Client 2 ──stdio───── Server B (database)
  └── Client 3 ──HTTP────── Server C (Sentry, remote)
```

## Protocol Layers

### Data Layer

The inner layer implements JSON-RPC 2.0 message exchange:

- **Lifecycle management** — Connection initialization, capability negotiation, termination
- **Server primitives** — Tools, resources, prompts exposed by servers
- **Client primitives** — Sampling (LLM completions), elicitation (user input), logging
- **Notifications** — Real-time updates when tool/resource/prompt lists change

### Transport Layer

The outer layer handles communication channels:

- **stdio** — Standard input/output for local subprocess communication. No network overhead. Client launches server as a child process.
- **Streamable HTTP** — HTTP POST for client-to-server messages, optional SSE for streaming. Supports remote servers, session management, and OAuth authorization.

See [[mcp/transport]] for transport details.

## Connection Lifecycle

Every MCP session follows this sequence:

### 1. Initialization

Client sends `initialize` with protocol version and its capabilities:

```json
{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "id": 1,
  "method": "initialize",
  "params": {
    "protocolVersion": "2025-06-18",
    "capabilities": {
      "elicitation": {}
    },
    "clientInfo": {
      "name": "my-client",
      "version": "1.0.0"
    }
  }
}
```

### 2. Server Response

Server responds with its capabilities:

```json
{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "id": 1,
  "result": {
    "protocolVersion": "2025-06-18",
    "capabilities": {
      "tools": { "listChanged": true },
      "resources": { "subscribe": true, "listChanged": true },
      "prompts": { "listChanged": true }
    },
    "serverInfo": {
      "name": "my-server",
      "version": "1.0.0"
    }
  }
}
```

### 3. Initialized Notification

Client confirms readiness:

```json
{ "jsonrpc": "2.0", "method": "notifications/initialized" }
```

### 4. Normal Operation

Client discovers and uses primitives: `tools/list`, `tools/call`, `resources/list`, `resources/read`, `prompts/list`, `prompts/get`.

### 5. Notifications

Server sends change notifications when primitives update:

```json
{ "jsonrpc": "2.0", "method": "notifications/tools/list_changed" }
```

## Capability Negotiation

Capabilities declared during initialization determine available features:

| Server Capability | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `tools` | Server exposes callable tools |
| `tools.listChanged` | Server sends tool list change notifications |
| `resources` | Server exposes readable resources |
| `resources.subscribe` | Clients can subscribe to resource changes |
| `resources.listChanged` | Server sends resource list change notifications |
| `prompts` | Server exposes prompt templates |
| `prompts.listChanged` | Server sends prompt list change notifications |

| Client Capability | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `sampling` | Client can handle LLM completion requests from server |
| `elicitation` | Client can request user input on behalf of server |

## Message Types

MCP uses three JSON-RPC 2.0 message types:

- **Request** — Has `id`, `method`, `params`. Expects a response.
- **Response** — Has `id`, `result` or `error`. Correlates to a request.
- **Notification** — Has `method`, no `id`. Fire-and-forget, no response expected.

## Protocol Version

The current protocol version is `2025-06-18`. Clients and servers negotiate a compatible version during initialization. If versions are incompatible, the connection should be terminated.

sections.title: Hosts, clients, and servers
sections.body: The host is the AI app (Claude Desktop, VS Code). It creates one MCP client per server. Each client maintains a dedicated JSON-RPC session. Servers expose primitives. Local servers use stdio. Remote servers use Streamable HTTP. A single remote server can serve multiple clients simultaneously.

sections.title: Data and transport layers
sections.body: The data layer defines JSON-RPC message semantics: lifecycle, primitives, notifications. The transport layer handles framing and communication: stdio for local processes, Streamable HTTP for remote. Both layers are independent — the same JSON-RPC messages work over any transport. See [[mcp/transport]].

sections.title: Connection lifecycle
sections.body: 1) Client sends `initialize` with protocol version and capabilities. 2) Server responds with its capabilities and server info. 3) Client sends `notifications/initialized`. 4) Normal primitive operations begin. 5) Either party can terminate. The initialization handshake is mandatory — no operations are valid before it completes.

sections.title: Capability negotiation
sections.body: Both client and server declare capabilities during initialization. Server capabilities: `tools`, `resources`, `prompts`, with optional `listChanged` and `subscribe` flags. Client capabilities: `sampling` (LLM completions), `elicitation` (user input). Only negotiated capabilities can be used during the session.

sections.title: Real-time notifications
sections.body: Servers send JSON-RPC notifications (no `id` field) when primitive lists change: `notifications/tools/list_changed`, `notifications/resources/list_changed`, `notifications/prompts/list_changed`. For subscribed resources: `notifications/resources/updated`. Clients re-fetch updated lists upon receiving notifications. This keeps the AI model's available capabilities current.
